pattern: Resolve each '?' left to right using only three candidate letters, since any single fixed neighbor can rule out at most one of three choices.
hints: You only ever need to compare a '?' against its immediate left and right neighbors, so a small fixed palette of candidate letters is enough — you don't need all 26 letters.
Fill '?'s left to right; once you've decided the character to its left, that decision plus the original right-hand character are the only two constraints on the current position.

Try 'a', then 'b', then 'c' for each '?', in that order, and pick the first candidate that differs from the just-resolved left neighbor and the original right neighbor — one of the three always works.

edge_inputs.description: no '?' at all, output must equal the input unchanged
a '?' sandwiched between two identical fixed letters must pick a third letter
'?' at the very start only has a right neighbor to satisfy
'?' at the very end only has a left neighbor to satisfy
a run of consecutive '?'s where each resolution constrains the next
single-character string that is itself a '?'

stress.description: random mix of letters and '?' with no pre-existing adjacent fixed repeats
stress.note: avoids generating two adjacent fixed characters that are already equal, since no '?' could fix that
